text.title: Hourly-to-Fixed Transition Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that govern a controlled hourly-to-fixed transition. These rules prevent the most common revenue-collapse pattern: simultaneous re-pricing of all clients.

rule.statement: Convert the single highest-margin existing client first. Margin = (current monthly revenue from client) ÷ (operator hours delivered) compared to target effective hourly. Reject pilots that start with the lowest-margin or newest client.
rule.rationale: First conversion has the highest motivation cost — fear of losing the relationship dominates. Starting with the client where you're already over-earning hourly reduces both the financial and emotional risk of refusal.

rule.statement: Maintain 2-3 hourly clients during the pilot fixed engagement. Do not convert > 1 client per month during the first quarter of transition.
rule.rationale: Hourly clients are the cash floor while fixed-price economics stabilize. Converting all clients in one round creates a 4-8 week trough that few solo operators survive without external runway.

rule.statement: Identify exactly ONE repeatable scope (audit, migration, integration, sprint) appearing in ≥ 3 past engagements. Productize that scope into a fixed SKU with explicit deliverables and an exclusions list. Do not productize 2+ SKUs in the same quarter.
rule.rationale: Multiple new SKUs split positioning, marketing, and operator focus. One SKU with sharp deliverables outsells a menu of three for the first 6-12 months.

rule.statement: After the pilot fixed engagement closes successfully, re-price all remaining hourly clients in a single one-week communication window. No staggered re-pricing across multiple months.
rule.rationale: Staggered re-pricing leaks across the client network ("Why did Acme get $200/hr and I got $150?"). One round, same effective date, same logic — fairness and finality.

rule.statement: Before starting the transition, document a 6-8 week cash bridge covering trough revenue. Bridge = savings + accessible credit + signed-pipeline revenue scheduled within the trough window. Reject any transition plan with bridge < 6 weeks AT operator's monthly burn.
rule.rationale: Operators forced to abort mid-transition (because of cash, not strategy) end up with worse pricing than where they started. The bridge is non-negotiable.

example.title: High-margin first conversion
example.code:
Client portfolio (monthly): Acme: $6k rev / 20 hrs = $300/hr effective. Target: $250/hr. MARGIN +20% Beta: $4k rev / 30 hrs = $133/hr effective. Target: $250/hr. MARGIN -47% Gamma: $3k rev / 15 hrs = $200/hr effective. Target: $250/hr. MARGIN -20%
Decision (r1): convert Acme first. Acme client is already paying above target, so fixed-price conversion is a relabeling, not a price hike.
example.note: Illustrates r1: highest-margin client = lowest-friction first convert.

example.title: Premature all-at-once transition
example.code:
Operator: "I'm going fixed-price across the board next month."
Result: Week 1-2: Two clients reject the conversion. Hourly revenue drops $7k/mo. Week 3-6: Productized SKU not yet selling. Pipeline at zero. Week 7: Operator takes hourly work back at LOWER rate to survive. Week 10: Transition aborted. Net position: worse than starting point.
example.note: Illustrates r2 + r5 violation. Simultaneous conversion + no cash bridge = forced retreat.
